title: Swarm stores all data as 4KB content-addressed chunks in a Merkle hash tree
content:
## Claim

Swarm's unit of storage is a **4KB content-addressed chunk (CAC)**. All data, regardless of size, is split into these chunks and reassembled via a Merkle hash tree.

## Evidence

- Max payload per chunk: 4096 bytes
- Chunk address = `Keccak256(span || BMT_root(payload))` where span is an 8-byte total-length prefix
- Self-verifying: any peer can recompute the address from the bytes
- Files larger than 4KB form a **hash tree**: leaf chunks hold data, intermediate chunks hold 128 child references (32 bytes each)
- The root chunk's address is the file's reference (32 bytes)
- Encrypted files drop branching factor to 64 because each ref becomes 64 bytes (32-byte address + 32-byte decryption key)

## Why it matters

The adapter uploads encrypted JSON via `POST /bytes`; the Bee node handles chunking automatically. We never touch raw chunks directly, but understanding this layer explains why reference sizes differ between plain and encrypted uploads.
description: Every Swarm upload is split into 4KB chunks whose addresses are Keccak256(span || BMT_root). Files >4KB form a Merkle tree rooted at a single reference hash.
